1: A Prayer of Moses the man of God — the only psalm attributed to Moses in the entire Psalter. The attribution is debated by critical scholars but the heading is early; the psalm was received as Mosaic in the Second Temple period. It opens Book IV (Ps 90-106), the collection that functions as a theological response to the crisis of the Davidic monarchy's collapse and the Babylonian exile. Book IV provides an answer to the lament of Ps 89: even when the Davidic covenant seems to have failed, YHWH has been Israel's maon (dwelling place, home, refuge) across all generations. The psalm's setting is therefore Israel after the exile, reading a Mosaic prayer that relocates identity from land and temple to YHWH himself.
2: Before the mountains were born — the cosmos-founding language that precedes the existence of the most enduring features of the known world. Mountains in the ancient Near East were the oldest things imaginable: the pillars of the sky, the dwelling places of gods, older than human memory. YHWH predates them all. The phrase me'olam ad olam (from age to age / from everlasting to everlasting) brackets the divine existence at both ends of time — no beginning accessible to human thought, no end conceivable. This is the psalm's theological premise: the eternal God is the only stable home for creatures whose own existence is radically impermanent.
3: You turn human beings back to dust — the reversal of the creation act of Genesis 2:7 (YHWH formed Adam from the dust of the ground) and the covenant curse of Genesis 3:19 ("dust you are and to dust you will return"). The divine command "Return, O children of Adam" (benei adam) echoes both the creation narrative and the judgment. The psalm places human transience within the divine governance: mortality is not a biological accident but an expression of YHWH's sovereign ordering of human life. The repeated address to "children of Adam" (generic humanity, not specifically Israel) gives the psalm a universal anthropological scope.
4: A thousand years in your sight are like yesterday after it has passed — the divine perspective on time: a millennium is experientially equivalent to the receding past of a single day or a night watch (approximately three hours). This is not a statement about the unreality of time but about the difference between creaturely and divine modes of experiencing it. The comparison "like yesterday after it has passed" (ke'etmol ki ya'avor) specifies a yesterday that is already past — not vivid yesterday but the fading, already-receding yesterday. Peter applies this specifically to the delay of the parousia (2 Peter 3:8), using it to counter impatient demands for immediate eschatological action.
5: You sweep them away like a sudden flood — the first of several images for the brevity and vulnerability of human life. The flood-metaphor implies overwhelming force and complete loss of control: humans are swept away by YHWH's action, not by mere biological process. The dream-imagery that follows points to the evanescent quality of life: a dream is vivid during experience but disappears completely upon waking. The grass metaphor begins here and is developed through v6. This cluster of images — flood, dream, grass — is a recognized literary topos in ANE wisdom literature (compare Job 14:1-2) for the fragility of human existence.
6: In the morning it blossoms and shoots up; by evening it is mown down and dry — the grass metaphor spans a single day as the compressed image of a complete human life: birth-to-death within the span of morning-to-evening. This is the same image that Isaiah 40:6-8 uses for the contrast between human transience and YHWH's eternal word: "All people are like grass... the grass withers and the flowers fall, but the word of our God stands forever." The Psalter and the prophets draw on the same wisdom image to locate human brevity within divine permanence. The psalm will later (v12) turn this observation from despair into practical wisdom.
7: We are brought to an end by your anger — the crucial shift: this psalm is not merely philosophical reflection on mortality but a covenantal lament. The human brevity described in vv 5-6 is specifically framed as the effect of divine anger (aph) and fury (hemah). The "we" is the covenant community. The psalm does not say that human transience is simply the result of finitude; it says it is the context within which YHWH's anger operates. This locates the psalm within the covenant-lawsuit tradition: Israel under the consequences of unfaithfulness, experiencing the divine displeasure that the Torah described as the covenant curse (Deuteronomy 28:15-68).
8: You have placed our sins before you and our hidden faults in the full light of your presence — the divine omniscience as it bears on judgment. "Hidden faults" (alumenu) — those wrongs the community has kept from public view, the sins concealed from other humans but not from YHWH. The same principle that Psalms 64:5 condemns in the wicked ("who will see us?") is here confessed about the community itself: the assumption of hiddenness is false. The "full light of your presence" (lim'or panekha) — the face-light of divine presence — serves as the ultimate revealer. No concealment survives the divine gaze.
9: All our days slip away under your wrath — the entire lifespan framed by divine displeasure. "We bring our years to an end like a breath" — the Hebrew word is hegeh, which can mean a sigh, a groan, or the low murmur of meditation. The years pass with the sound of a sigh: not even a cry, just an exhaled breath. The image is of a life that does not end with fanfare but simply dissipates. This verse has moved from the anger of vv 7-8 to the ordinary passage of time: even lives not marked by dramatic judgment simply sigh away. The wrath is not periodic punishment but the ambient condition of creaturely existence under divine assessment.
10: The span of our years is seventy — or if we are strong, eighty — not a biological claim but a wisdom observation. In the ancient world, many died far younger (infant mortality was extremely high); the psalm treats seventy and eighty as the upper range of normal life expectancy. Notably, Moses himself lived 120 years (Deuteronomy 34:7) and is recorded as vigorous to the end — the psalm's putative author was an exception to the rule he describes, which is part of its dramatic force. "Only toil and grief" (amal va'aven) — even the strong years at 80 contain only labor and trouble. The psalm does not romanticize longevity: more years means more accumulated suffering.
11: Who truly grasps the force of your anger? — a rhetorical question that implies the answer is "almost no one." The "force" (oz) of divine anger is not grasped by ordinary human perception; only the one who fears YHWH as the divine wrath deserves to be feared is able to perceive it accurately. This is a wisdom observation: the failure to fear YHWH properly is itself evidence of a distorted perception of reality. The fear of YHWH (yirah) that properly calibrates the response to divine anger is the beginning of wisdom (Proverbs 1:7; Psalms 9:10) — and its absence is evidence of the fool's miscalibrated worldview.
12: Teach us, then, to count our days aright — the pivot from lament to petition. The observation of human transience (vv 3-11) now generates a specific request: teach us to number our days, to count them one by one with the awareness that each is finite and irreplaceable. "That we may acquire a wise heart" (levav chokmah) — wisdom is not the natural result of aging but the outcome of deliberate attention to mortality. The wisdom tradition (Proverbs 9:10; Ecclesiastes 12:1-7) consistently treats the awareness of death as the catalyst for wisdom rather than as an occasion for either denial or despair. This is the psalm's practical anthropology.
13: Turn back, LORD! How long will this go on? — shuvah addressed to YHWH: the standard covenant lament formula asking God to turn back to his people (Psalms 6:4; Psalms 80:14; Lamentations 5:21). The "how long" (ad matai) is the temporal cry of the lament psalms — the open-ended question that expresses the felt endlessness of the current distress without denying the expectation that it will end. "Have mercy on your servants" — the community identifies as YHWH's avadim (servants), the covenant relational term that the prayer returns to at vv 16 and 17. The appeal is to covenant relationship, not to human merit.
14: Fill us in the morning with your steadfast love — hesed in the morning: the same pattern as Psalms 92:2 (declaring steadfast love each morning) and Lamentations 3:22-23 ("his mercies are new every morning"). The "morning" is the time of rescue in the Psalter — the darkness of night is the time of distress and the morning is when YHWH acts (Psalms 30:5; Psalms 46:5; Psalms 130:5-6). The prayer asks for hesed-saturation (male, fill us) at the beginning of each day so that the day's experience is shaped by covenant love rather than by the awareness of divine wrath from vv 7-11. The joy and celebration that follow are the natural fruits of hesed-saturation.
15: Give us gladness to match the days you have made us suffer — the precise proportionality of the request: not merely some gladness but gladness (simhah) equivalent in measure to the years of affliction. This is the same logic as YHWH's response to Job: double restoration (Job 42:10). It is also the eschatological promise of Isaiah 61:7 ("instead of their shame my people will receive a double portion... everlasting joy will be theirs"). The prayer does not ask YHWH to minimize the past suffering but to match it with future joy — a redemptive transformation of the ledger, not an erasure. The "years" of trouble are still countable; the years of joy must count at least as many.
16: Let your work appear to your servants — YHWH's po'al (work/deed) made visible to the community. In the context of exile, this prayer asks for visible signs of divine action in history — the kind of demonstrable intervention that the Exodus generation saw (the psalm's Mosaic attribution connects the community's prayer to the paradigm of visible divine action). "Your splendor shine on their children" — the next generation: the prayer spans beyond the present moment to the children of those now suffering. The continuity of divine favor across generations is the heart of covenant blessing (Deuteronomy 7:9; Psalms 103:17-18).
17: May the favor of the LORD our God rest upon us — no'am YHWH (the pleasantness/beauty/favor of YHWH) as the crowning request. This is the rare word used in Psalms 27:4 for the single thing David desires: "to gaze on the beauty of the LORD." The favor of divine presence is the answer to the psalm's opening claim: YHWH is our dwelling (v1) and our request is that his favor may "rest upon us" (alenu). "Establish the work of our hands" — the double repetition ("establish... yes, establish") is emphatic and liturgical. Human labor becomes meaningful only when it is taken up and made permanent by the divine act of establishment. The psalm ends not in resignation but in confident petition.
